description: Five identity group case studies documenting exclusive inclusion across different demographic categories. Each case documents the empirical evidence, governance problem, and jurisdictional retreat mechanism.

common_structural_pattern.claim: The mechanism is architectural, not attitudinal, and operates simultaneously across all five identity groups.
common_structural_pattern.description: Across Women of Color in STEM, Black students at liberal arts colleges, faculty with non-conforming or dissenting views, Jewish students, and students with disabilities, the same governance architecture yields exclusion because the accountability void does not discriminate among the groups it leaves unprotected.
common_structural_pattern.implication: Governance reform benefits every affected constituency because it targets the shared structural mechanism rather than group-specific attitudes.

cases.headline: Campus Antisemitism Post-October 7, 2023
cases.trigger: The Hamas attack on Israel on October 7, 2023 precipitated a wave of campus demonstrations, encampments, exclusion zones, faculty boycott endorsements, and systematic intimidation of Jewish students at universities with explicit anti-discrimination policies.

empirical_data.source: Waldman-Levi et al. (2025) national survey of Jewish college students

key_statistics.stat: 57.3% of Jewish students reported direct personal experiences of antisemitism
key_statistics.significance: Majority of Jewish students experienced antisemitism firsthand — not merely ambient hostility

key_statistics.stat: 63.4% of Jewish students reported bystander exposure to antisemitism
key_statistics.significance: Supermajority of Jewish students witnessed antisemitism directed at others

key_statistics.stat: Most Jewish students lacked confidence their universities would respond to reported antisemitism
key_statistics.significance: Institutional non-response documented by affected students themselves — not merely asserted by critics

institutional_data.incidents: 4,130 antisemitism incidents reported; increase of 3,150 from prior year
Faculty boycott endorsers held academic leadership positions shaping curricula, pedagogy, and speaker selection through captured governance structures

empirical_data.federal_response: Federal officials launched Title VI investigations into more than sixty institutions. Nine-figure settlements extracted from Columbia, Brown, and others. Conditions imposed: definitions of antisemitism, liaisons, climate surveys. No settlement required governance reform.

additional_sources.citation: Sharon Avni & Nicole Samuel, 'An Unprecedented Year in Jewish Education and American Jewish Life: Antisemitism on Campus After October 7, 2023,' Journal of Jewish Education, 91(2), 103-106 (2025), DOI: 10.1080/15244113.2025.2496588
additional_sources.methodology: Scholarly synthesis and editorial overview of peer-reviewed research on antisemitism on campus following October 7, 2023; introduces special issue of the Journal of Jewish Education
key_findings: The October 7, 2023 Hamas attack sent shockwaves through Jewish communities immediately, making American college and university campuses 'ground zero' for campus conflict
Students and faculty on both sides of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict reacted with grief, fear, and anger; institutional responses were inconsistent and contested
The article frames the volume as documenting 'an unprecedented year' in Jewish education — acknowledging that the scale of documented harm exceeded historical comparators
University responses exposed institutional inability to enforce anti-discrimination commitments, consistent with the governance void thesis
additional_sources.relevance_to_governance_void: Avni & Samuel (2025) provides peer-reviewed framing for the Jewish student case study's central empirical claim: that October 7 revealed, rather than created, the governance void that left Jewish students structurally unprotected despite formal inclusion commitments.

governance_problem.description: At multiple University of California campuses, departmental units used delegated authority to advance coordinated campaigns that fueled exclusion and intimidation of Jewish student groups. The Board of Regents had no governance mechanism to intervene in departmental conduct.
governance_problem.encampment_governance_failure: Encampment operations at UCLA created exclusion zones from which Jewish students were physically blocked. University administration claimed inability to intervene without faculty senate approval. Faculty senate claimed the conduct was protected expression. Neither body had enforceable authority to compel the other to act.
governance_problem.faculty_capture: Faculty boycott endorsers held academic leadership positions at UCLA, UC Berkeley, and UC Santa Cruz, shaping curricula, pedagogy, and speaker selection through captured governance structures. No mechanism existed to challenge departmental decisions through internal channels.
governance_problem.asymmetry: Universities maintained offices, programs, and enforcement mechanisms for racial equity, gender inclusion, LGBTQ+ advocacy, and disability access. The same universities treated identity-based exclusion of Jewish students as political expression falling outside civil rights infrastructure — demonstrating inclusion asymmetry.

jurisdictional_retreat.mechanism: Faculty and student organizations claiming individual academic freedom or political expression to shield institutional deployment of departmental authority against Jewish students
jurisdictional_retreat.example: Faculty for Justice in Palestine chapters used departmental authority, budgets, and speaker-selection power to exclude Israeli and Jewish scholars; individual faculty members claimed personal academic freedom when challenged. Institutional character disappeared from the analysis.
jurisdictional_retreat.result: Without stakeholder standing, affected Jewish students had no internal mechanism to challenge departmental decisions. Title VI complaints required external federal process lasting months to years. Governance void left them structurally unprotected.

cases.why_governance_void_explains: The same governance architecture that makes universities unable to enforce their anti-discrimination commitments against antisemitism also makes them unable to enforce those commitments for every other identity group. The governing body answers to no one; the fiduciary duty has no enforcement mechanism; no affected student can bring a derivative claim.

cases.headline: Race-Avoidant Governance in Computer Science Gender Equity Programs

empirical_data.source: Documented at four public research universities with formal gender equity programs in computer science departments
empirical_data.key_finding: Computer science departments with formal gender equity programs systematically excluded Women of Color through race-avoidant governance processes that prioritized gender while erasing race.
empirical_data.mechanism_detail: Institutional DEI roles were narrowly defined around gender diversity; race was excluded from the operational definition of 'diversity' in program design, metrics, and eligibility. Women of Color were formally included in the category 'women' but excluded from the practices that substantively addressed barriers to inclusion.
empirical_data.pattern: Four separate institutions with similar formal commitments produced the same exclusionary pattern — consistent with the hypothesis that governance architecture, not individual actors, generates the outcome

primary_source.citation: Julia Rose Karpicz, Tomoko M. Nakajima & Justin A. Gutzwa, 'Challenging Normalized Gendered Racism in Departmental Efforts to Broaden Participation in Computer Science,' Journal of Women and Gender in Higher Education, 17(4), 357-379 (2024), DOI: 10.1080/26379112.2024.2373479
primary_source.journal: Journal of Women and Gender in Higher Education

methodology.design: Multi-site qualitative study examining race-avoidant governance in four public research universities with formal gender equity programs in computer science departments
methodology.sites: Four public research universities with formal gender equity programs in CS
methodology.theoretical_framework: Gendered racism framework; institutional analysis of how race and gender intersect in DEI program design
methodology.data_collection: Interviews with program participants, faculty, and administrators; document analysis of program materials and policies

key_findings: Computer science departments with formal gender equity programs systematically excluded Women of Color through race-avoidant governance processes
Institutional DEI roles were narrowly defined around gender; race was excluded from the operational definition of 'diversity' in program design, metrics, and eligibility
Women of Color were formally included in the category 'women' but excluded from practices substantively addressing barriers to inclusion — a textbook exclusive inclusion pattern
Four separate institutions with similar formal commitments produced the same exclusionary pattern — consistent with governance architecture as the causal mechanism, not individual actors

Post-SFFA (Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard, 600 U.S. 181 (2023)) institutional caution created incentives to design programs claiming race-neutrality; Women of Color bore the cost of institutions optimizing for legal defensibility over substantive inclusion
The article introduces the term 'gendered racism' to capture the intersection that race-avoidant DEI programs systematically erase
primary_source.governance_void_connection: The Karpicz et al. study documents the second-order diversity mechanism: each individual programmatic decision appeared legally defensible in isolation; the aggregate pattern was systematic exclusion of Women of Color. No single decision-maker could be held accountable for the pattern — the disaggregated governance architecture prevented accountability.

governance_problem.description: DEI committee structures were designed to address gender; they lacked authority or mandate to address the intersection of race and gender. Faculty and administrators with decision-making authority over program design had no accountability mechanism requiring them to address race-gender intersection.
governance_problem.race_avoidance: Post-SFFA (Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard, 600 U.S. 181 (2023)) institutional caution created incentives to design programs that could claim race-neutrality. Women of Color bore the cost of institutions optimizing for legal defensibility over substantive inclusion.
governance_problem.second_order_dynamic: Each individual programmatic decision appeared defensible in isolation; the aggregate pattern was systematic exclusion of Women of Color. No single decision-maker could be held accountable for the pattern — the governance architecture disaggregated responsibility.

jurisdictional_retreat.mechanism: Institutional reframing: when Women of Color raised the intersection issue, program administrators characterized the gender equity program as legally constrained, citing post-SFFA uncertainty; individual faculty members exercised 'academic judgment' in program design; no institutional actor claimed responsibility for the aggregate exclusionary pattern.
jurisdictional_retreat.result: Affected Women of Color had no standing to challenge DEI committee decisions, no enforceable standard to invoke, and no institutional pathway for accountability. External agencies faced coordination problems identifying which actor had produced the pattern.

cases.why_governance_void_explains: Gender equity programs with formal commitments produced systematic exclusion of Women of Color because the governance void — self-perpetuating committee structure, unenforceable mission, no stakeholder standing — allowed the programs to optimize for legal defensibility rather than substantive inclusion with no accountability for the resulting harm.

cases.identity_group: Black Students at Predominantly White Liberal Arts Colleges
cases.headline: Race-Neutral Behavioral Policy Reproducing Racial Inequality

primary_study.citation: Alanna Gillis & Elena G. van Stee, 'The Pact: How a Seemingly Race-Neutral Behavioral Policy Reproduced Racial Inequality at a Predominantly White Liberal Arts College,' Socius: Sociological Research for a Dynamic World, Vol. 10 (2024), DOI: 10.1177/23780231241286371
primary_study.methodology: Three waves of in-depth interviews with 30 undergraduates (N = 75 interviews) at a selective liberal arts college (SLAC); longitudinal design covering the 2020-2021 academic year
primary_study.institution: Predominantly White selective liberal arts college (SLAC); historically catered to wealthy White population; recently expanded through Higher Education Opportunity Project (HEOP) recruiting lower-income students of color from New York City, primarily Black and Latinx students on (nearly) full financial aid

empirical_data.the_policy: The Pact: an ostensibly race-neutral COVID-19 behavioral policy implemented to reduce outbreaks by limiting students' close contacts. The policy succeeded in its epidemiological goal while inadvertently imposing disproportionate social costs on SLAC's small population of students of color.

four_disparity_domains.finding: Residential assignment patterns, combined with the Pact's contact restrictions, isolated students of color from peer networks while White students retained access to established social networks rooted in shared class background

White students, particularly varsity athletes and affluent students, had access to informal networks and physical spaces that allowed rule-bending without exposure to institutional sanctions; students of color lacked equivalent access to these informal exemptions

Black male students experienced heightened surveillance both on and off campus; their rule violations were more visible and more likely to be reported; differential enforcement produced disparate outcomes from nominally identical rules

Students of color faced higher stakes for policy violations — more likely to lose financial aid, housing, or academic standing — while White students faced lower formal and informal consequences for comparable violations

three_mechanisms: Unequal resource allocation: SLAC's demographic composition (students of color clustered at the bottom of the socioeconomic distribution) meant equal formal policy produced unequal substantive impact
Uneven rule enforcement: differential enforcement patterns produced disparate outcomes from nominally identical rules
Color-blind decision-making: policy-makers designed the Pact without analyzing racial impacts, treating race-neutrality as sufficient for racial equity
empirical_data.institutional_characteristics: SLAC's majority-White demographics, privileged treatment of varsity athletes, and tight coupling of racial and class inequality were identified as specific organizational characteristics that facilitated the observed patterns

governance_problem.description: The college's DEI commitments were formal, not structural. The Pact was designed without racial impact analysis because the governance structure contained no mechanism requiring such analysis as a precondition for policy adoption.
governance_problem.explicit_dei_failure: The college had explicit DEI commitments. Those commitments did not prevent the adoption of a policy that systematically disadvantaged students of color, because the governance structure contained no mechanism for enforcing the commitments against individual policy decisions.
governance_problem.color_blind_governance: Color-blind decision-making — treating the absence of explicit racial distinctions as sufficient to satisfy DEI commitments — was not an individual failure but a governance design feature: no standing, no standard, no mechanism.

jurisdictional_retreat.mechanism: When students of color raised the disparate impact of the Pact, administrators characterized the policy as race-neutral (accurate in design, inaccurate in effect) and therefore not subject to DEI review; individual faculty and administrators exercised 'neutral' academic and administrative judgment; the color-blind framing disabled the institution's civil rights infrastructure.
jurisdictional_retreat.griggs_relevance: Griggs v. Duke Power Co. disparate impact analysis would reach the Pact's effects: a facially neutral policy producing systematic racial disadvantage is actionable without proof of discriminatory intent. But students of color had no standing to invoke Griggs-based analysis at the institutional level; only the government could bring such a claim, and no government actor did so.
jurisdictional_retreat.result: Students of color affected by the Pact had no internal governance mechanism to challenge the policy, no enforceable standard to invoke, and no legal standing to bring a claim. The governance void left them structurally unprotected despite the college's formal DEI commitments.

cases.why_governance_void_explains: The Pact case is the clearest laboratory demonstration of exclusive inclusion: an institution with explicit DEI commitments, staffed by individuals who sincerely supported those commitments, produced systematic racial inequality as a structural output of color-blind policy design. The governance void — no stakeholder standing, hollow duty of obedience, self-perpetuating governance — prevented internal course-correction.

cases.identity_group: Faculty with Non-Conforming or Dissenting Views
cases.headline: Ideological Conformity Through Governance Capture of Hiring and Evaluation
cases.terminology_note: The operative category is 'faculty with non-conforming or dissenting views' — faculty whose intellectual positions deviate from the dominant institutional consensus — not partisan conservatives. The mechanism focuses on institutional consensus enforcement, not partisan valence. The FIRE data documents the current asymmetry but the governance mechanism would operate symmetrically against any minority-view faculty at any ideologically homogeneous institution.

empirical_data.source: Noah Honeycutt, 'Silence in the Classroom: The 2024 FIRE Faculty Survey Report,' Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (2024) [hereinafter FIRE 2024 Report]
empirical_data.survey_scope: 6,269 tenured, tenure-track, and non-tenure track faculty at 55 four-year colleges and universities; described as the largest survey of faculty ever conducted on this topic

key_statistics.stat: 35% of faculty have recently toned down their writing for fear of controversy
key_statistics.comparison: 9% of social scientists reported the same during the McCarthy era — a Cold War period of substantial pressure to restrict faculty expression
key_statistics.significance: Current self-censorship rate is nearly four times the McCarthyism baseline

key_statistics.stat: 14% of faculty suffered discipline or threats of discipline for their teaching, research, academic talks, or other off-campus speech
key_statistics.significance: Approximately 1 in 7 faculty report concrete disciplinary consequences or threats — not merely ambient fear

key_statistics.stat: 27% of faculty feel unable to speak freely for fear of how students, administrators, or other faculty would respond
key_statistics.significance: More than 1 in 4 faculty report a chilling effect sufficient to alter their speech behavior

key_statistics.stat: 40% of faculty worry about damaging their reputations because someone misunderstands something they have said or done
23% of faculty worry about losing their jobs because someone misunderstands something they have said or done
key_statistics.significance: Approximately 1 in 4 faculty — roughly paralleling the rate of department hostility

key_statistics.stat: 23% say their own departments are 'somewhat' or 'very' hostile toward people with their political beliefs
key_statistics.significance: Departmental hostility reported by 1 in 4 faculty; locates the mechanism at the subunit governance level

key_statistics.stat: 55% of conservative faculty at least occasionally hide their political views to keep their jobs, compared to 17% of liberal faculty
key_statistics.significance: Asymmetry is three-to-one; behavioral adaptation confirms the chilling effect is not merely attitudinal

key_statistics.stat: Nearly three-quarters of faculty said a liberal individual would be a positive fit in their department; only 1 in 5 said the same about a conservative individual
key_statistics.significance: 'Poor fit' translates operationally to 'not admitted,' 'not hired,' or 'not promoted' — documenting governance capture of hiring and evaluation

key_statistics.stat: 50% of faculty say it is rarely or never justified to require faculty job candidates to submit DEI commitment statements
key_statistics.significance: Majority of faculty oppose DEI loyalty tests yet the practice persists — consistent with governance capture of hiring committees over faculty majority preference

key_statistics.stat: 66% of faculty say colleges and universities should not take positions on political and social issues
key_statistics.significance: Supermajority of faculty support institutional neutrality (Kalven principle); institutional political positioning persists despite faculty majority preference — consistent with governance capture

illustrative_case.subject: Johnson Varkey, biology professor, St. Philip's College
illustrative_case.facts: Varkey explained chromosomal sex determination during his Human Anatomy and Physiology class in November 2022; four students walked out; college placed him under investigation for an ethics violation; terminated his contract; reinstated after FIRE intervention one year later
illustrative_case.significance: Documents how individual faculty members with non-conforming views face institutional discipline through captured governance structures; process resolved only through external legal intervention

kathleen_mcelroy_case.subject: Kathleen Oveta McElroy, Texas A&M journalism program hire
kathleen_mcelroy_case.facts: Hired to head revived journalism program in June 2023; administration and board of regents members complained about her DEI background and race research; interim dean told her certain people might force leadership to fire her; McElroy walked away; A&M investigated; interim dean and president resigned; McElroy settled for $1 million; internal report confirmed emails from administrators and regents showing political issues over DEI played a role; Bermúdez told McElroy that being 'a black woman who worked at The New York Times' with a DEI background was a problem
kathleen_mcelroy_case.significance: Documents intersection of race and viewpoint exclusion operating through captured board governance; external accountability (financial settlement) resolved the individual case but not the governance structure

additional_sources.citation: Stanley Rothman, S. Robert Lichter & Neil Nevitte, 'Politics and Professional Advancement Among College Faculty,' The Forum, 3(1) (2005). DOI: 10.2202/1540-8884.1067

methodology.design: Nationally representative survey of college faculty
methodology.sample: 1,643 faculty members from 183 four-year colleges and universities; randomly sampled
methodology.variables: Ideological self-identification; professional accomplishment measures (publications, grants, institutional rank); institutional quality (Carnegie classification)

key_findings: Liberals and Democrats outnumber conservatives and Republicans in American college faculty by large margins; differences are not limited to elite universities or social sciences and humanities

Controlling for professional accomplishment and other individual characteristics, conservatives and Republicans teach at lower-quality institutions than liberals and Democrats — suggesting ideologically-based discrimination in professional advancement
Similar effects found for gender (women teach at lower-quality schools than professional accomplishments would predict) and religiosity (practicing Christians teach at lower-quality schools)
The correlation between ideology and institutional quality persists after controlling for professional merit, supporting the hypothesis that ideological homogeneity in academia is self-reinforcing
additional_sources.relevance: Provides early empirical foundation for the faculty ideological homogeneity thesis; methodology employs controls for professional merit to isolate the ideology-advancement correlation — directly relevant to the governance void theory's claim that the mechanism is structural rather than attitudinal.

additional_sources.citation: Richard E. Redding, 'Likes Attract: The Sociopolitical Groupthink of (Social) Psychologists,' Perspectives on Psychological Science, 7(5), 512-515 (2012), DOI: 10.1177/1745691612455206

methodology.design: Narrative review and synthesis of empirical literature on sociopolitical discrimination in academic psychology; responds to Inbar & Lammers (2012) empirical findings
methodology.theoretical_framework: Groupthink theory applied to sociopolitical discrimination; analysis of self-reinforcing exclusion mechanisms

key_findings: Empirical evidence documents substantial levels of discrimination against conservative professors and policy perspectives in the academy; this is not merely anecdotal

Sociopolitical discrimination is difficult to overcome because sociopolitical values are often a core component of self-identity — parallel to how racial or gender identity functions in discrimination analysis

Tendency to marginalize the sociopolitical 'other' is coupled with groupthink that presupposes such discrimination is acceptable (e.g., because conservatives are self-interestedly motivated or conservatism is represented elsewhere)

The assumptions underlying promotion of racial, ethnic, gender, and sexual orientation diversity apply equally to sociopolitical diversity — making ideological exclusion analytically parallel to identity-group exclusion

Gartner (1986) mock admissions study: professors rated a religious conservative applicant less likely to be admitted to graduate programs — direct experimental evidence of discriminatory decision-making
additional_sources.relevance: Redding (2012) provides the analytical parallel that Exclusive Inclusion exploits: the same governance mechanisms that enable racial or gender exclusion also enable ideological exclusion. The structural argument transcends any single identity group.

additional_sources.citation: Keith E. Whittington, 'Intellectual Diversity Mandates and the Freedom to Teach' (SSRN Working Paper, 2024), ssrn.com/abstract=5057350
additional_sources.author: Keith E. Whittington, David Boies Professor of Law, Yale Law School; Founding Chair, Academic Freedom Alliance

methodology.design: Legal and policy analysis of Indiana Senate Bill 202 (adopted 2024), the first state intellectual diversity mandate imposed on public university classroom teaching
methodology.scope: Examines conceptual, normative, and practical problems with intellectual diversity mandates; considers constitutional dimensions of state legislative intervention in classroom instruction

key_findings: Indiana SB 202 is the first law to impose an 'intellectual diversity' mandate on public university classroom teaching; it is part of a larger movement of state legislative intervention
Intellectual diversity mandates pose significant threats to traditional principles of academic freedom — the freedom of university professors to teach in accord with their own professional judgment

The mandate is likely to lower the quality of university education while failing to accomplish its intended policy aims — demonstrating that state intervention as a reform mechanism is inferior to governance reform targeting the structural void
The article distinguishes intellectual diversity mandates (less dangerous) from 'divisive concepts' bans (more dangerous) but treats both as extraordinary political intrusions into faculty autonomy

Whittington's analysis of the freedom to teach anchors the academic freedom dimension of the faculty case study — the governance void creates conditions where both the Jurisdictional Retreat and external state intervention are used to suppress faculty with non-conforming views
additional_sources.relevance: Whittington (2024) establishes that legislative intellectual diversity mandates — the external reform mechanism — are inadequate substitutes for governance reform. This supports the Exclusive Inclusion argument that Reform 1 (stakeholder standing) and Reform 2 (mission specificity) are preferable to state content mandates because they work through governance accountability rather than speech compulsion.

governance_problem.description: Faculty hiring in many departments operates through informal culture and committee discretion that effectively screens out candidates whose views deviate from the dominant institutional consensus, substituting ideological conformity for open intellectual inquiry.
governance_problem.mechanism: Departmental hiring committees exercise subjective 'fit' judgments without enforceable standards; search processes lack stakeholder accountability; board oversight is nominal; external candidates with dissenting views are rejected through processes that generate no reviewable record.
governance_problem.institutional_claim: Universities claim to value intellectual diversity. Faculty senate majorities endorse institutional neutrality. The formal commitments are genuine. The governance structure — committee discretion, subjective fit standards, no external review, no stakeholder standing — makes those commitments structurally unenforceable at the department level.

jurisdictional_retreat.mechanism: Academic freedom and faculty governance autonomy: departmental decisions on hiring, promotion, tenure, and course assignments are characterized as faculty governance prerogatives immune from external review; 'academic judgment' forecloses challenge to the exclusionary pattern
jurisdictional_retreat.burt_v_gates_limit: Burt v. Rumsfeld (Gates), 354 F.Supp.2d 156 (D. Conn. 2005): The district court held that there is no substantive due process right of educational autonomy that extends to blocking equal access obligations. Applied to the institutional axis of the disentanglement matrix: academic freedom does not provide institutional immunity for systematically deploying official governance authority to exclude on identity or viewpoint grounds.
jurisdictional_retreat.result: Faculty with non-conforming views who are excluded through hiring, promotion, or assignment have no standing to challenge departmental decisions, no enforceable standard to invoke, and no mechanism for institutional accountability. The academic freedom that should protect dissent is invoked by the capturing faction to shield the exclusionary pattern.

cases.analytical_question: The governance mechanism for faculty with non-conforming views is structurally identical to the mechanism for other identity groups: committee capture, informal standards, no standing, hollow duty of obedience. The legal treatment differs: courts extend substantial deference to faculty governance decisions. Whether viewpoint exclusion is the 'same' governance mechanism as identity exclusion, and whether the legal asymmetry proves or undermines the cross-identity structural claim, is an open question the paper flags for workshop discussion.

cases.headline: Principal-Agent Breakdown Converting Legal Entitlements to Contingent Privileges

sources.finding: Accommodation processes exhibit substantial principal-agent breakdown; formal entitlements routinely not delivered
ADA and Section 504 legal entitlements for students with disabilities function as contingent privileges dependent on individual accommodation officers; systematic under-enforcement documented

empirical_data.pattern: Students with documented disabilities hold legal entitlements — accommodations required by ADA and Section 504 — that are systematically not delivered because the delivery mechanism depends on individual agent discretion without internal accountability structure.

additional_sources.citation: Megan MacKenzie, Özlem Sensoy, Genevieve Fuji Johnson, Nathalie Sinclair & Laurel Weldon, 'How Universities Gaslight EDI&I Initiatives: Mapping Institutional Resistance to Structural Change,' IJEPL, 19(1) (2023)
additional_sources.journal: International Journal of Education Policy and Leadership (IJEPL)

methodology.design: Qualitative case study of two EDI&I (Equity, Diversity, Inclusion, and Indigenization) initiatives at post-secondary institutions; close examination of 'pushback' against initiatives
methodology.theoretical_framework: Introduces concept of 'institutional gaslighting' — universities simultaneously embracing EDI&I discourse while deploying strategies preventing dismantling of systemic inequalities

key_findings: Despite visibility of EDI&I discourses within large institutions, research has documented only modest advancements on stated values
Blocks to advancement stem from the structural nature of racist and sexist domination, especially embeddedness in formal and informal institutional norms
Introduces concept of 'institutional gaslighting': universities paradoxically embrace EDI&I discourse while simultaneously deploying strategies preventing dismantling systemic inequalities

This gaslighting dynamic is designed to help identify forms of resistance in settings of high stated value — directly paralleling the governance void theory's explanation of inclusive language with exclusionary structural outcomes
additional_sources.relevance: MacKenzie et al. (2023) documents the institutional gaslighting dynamic from a different theoretical direction but converges on the same structural diagnosis as Exclusive Inclusion: formal EDI&I commitments do not produce substantive inclusion when governance architecture lacks accountability mechanisms. The 'institutional gaslighting' framing is an alternative theoretical vocabulary for the governance void.

governance_problem.description: Accommodation offices operate as the implementing agent for statutory disability rights. The principal-agent chain from board to disability services officer is long and unmonitored; each link has discretion; no stakeholder can compel delivery of the entitlement through internal governance channels.
governance_problem.principal_agent_breakdown: Board sets policy; president implements; dean oversees; disability services office administers; individual officers make delivery decisions. Each link introduces discretion; no link is subject to enforceable accountability from the student stakeholder.
governance_problem.structural_comparison: Unlike any other service that universities promise, disability accommodations have the distinctive feature of being both legally mandated and discretionarily administered — a combination that the governance void converts from entitlement to contingent privilege.
governance_problem.result: Students with disabilities who are denied required accommodations have no internal governance mechanism to enforce their rights; external complaint processes (OCR complaints, litigation) are expensive, slow, and impose substantial costs on students with already-impaired capacity to navigate adversarial processes.

jurisdictional_retreat.mechanism: Administrative discretion: accommodation decisions are characterized as clinical or educational judgments by trained professionals, insulated from external review; individual officers invoke expertise to shield non-delivery from accountability
jurisdictional_retreat.formal_legal_entitlement_gap: Students with disabilities have the most formally robust legal entitlements of any case study group (ADA, Section 504, institutional policy) and among the least effective governance mechanisms for enforcing them. The gap between formal entitlement and structural enforcement capacity demonstrates that formal legal rights without governance accountability mechanisms produce hollow protection — the exact conclusion the three structural reforms address.
jurisdictional_retreat.result: The disability case is analytically important precisely because it involves clear legal entitlements. When governance void can produce systematic non-delivery even of legally mandated services, it demonstrates that formal rights alone are insufficient; governance architecture is the mechanism of enforcement.

cases.why_governance_void_explains: The disability case isolates the governance void as the causal variable by controlling for legal entitlement: students with disabilities have strong formal rights and still face systematic exclusion because the governance structure contains no enforcement mechanism for the affected stakeholder. If formal legal rights were sufficient, the disability case would not exist. The governance void explains the gap.

hospital_community_benefit_source.citation: Michael D. Rozier, 'Nonprofit Hospital Community Benefit in the U.S.: A Scoping Review From 2010 to 2019,' Frontiers in Public Health, 8:72 (2020), DOI: 10.3389/fpubh.2020.00072

methodology.design: Scoping review of peer-reviewed literature published 2010-2019 on nonprofit hospital community benefit
methodology.search_strategy: OvidSP MEDLINE, PubMed, and SCOPUS searches; qualitative organization using axial coding methodology
methodology.scope: Governance, CHNA/CHIP process, community programs, spending patterns, population health, policy recommendations

key_findings: IRS Schedule H (2009) and ACA Section 501(r) (2010) significantly increased transparency and accountability for nonprofit hospital community benefit activities
Literature clustered around spending and needs assessments; governance is an area with relatively little known — consistent with the governance void thesis
Policy recommendations focus on accountability for impact, enhancing collaboration, and incentivizing action in areas other than clinical care
The 501(r) model demonstrates that operational governance accountability requirements are legally feasible for 501(c)(3) institutions — directly supporting Reform 3 (Operational Test)
hospital_community_benefit_source.relevance_to_reform_003: Rozier (2020) documents the existing hospital community benefit standard that serves as the legal model for Reform 3 (Operational Test). The article shows that the hospital accountability mechanism (IRS Schedule H + ACA Section 501(r) CHNAs) has produced measurable transparency gains and governance attention. The same mechanism, applied to universities, could condition 501(c)(3) status on demonstrated governance structures preventing systematic identity-based exclusion.
